recent_posts.text:
A few people have asked how Arga Labs (YC X26) differs from tools like Claude Code Review or the Claude Code Chrome extension.

Those tools operate at the application layer, they read through code changes in a PR or help verify built outputs in the browser.

But with modern LLMs, AI-generated changes increasingly span:
• infrastructure
• database schemas
• APIs and integrations
• multi-service wor

🤖 Agents are supposed to do the boring work for humans - not the other way around. But today, engineers still spend hours manually validating code generated by AI agents.

Introducing Arga Labs (YC X26)’s newest feature: AI agents that red-team your software, autonomously, across any tech stack.

In this demo, Arga launches a swarm of agents into a sandboxed clone of Discord. They explore and inte

Most people don’t know where Arga Labs (YC X26) got its name.

It comes from Argus Panoptes, the giant in Greek mythology with a hundred eyes. Even when he slept, some of them were always open.

That’s also why our logo is shaped like an eye.

AI agents can now write code, modify infrastructure, and respond to incidents. But someone still needs to watch the system.

We’re building the validation l

My “intern project” at Amazon last summer was a developer tool originally owned by an SDE II, and reassigned to me after a team restructure.

It ended up saving 10+ recurring engineering weeks per year by automating an internal developer workflow.

That experience shaped how I think about developer tooling.

Today, with AI coding agents, code generation is no longer the bottleneck.

Validation is.

AI can generate code faster than engineers can safely validate it.

That gap is becoming the biggest bottleneck in AI-driven development.
We wrote about why AI agents fail in production, and why validation is the missing layer. Link in the comments.

My Cofounder Akira Tong and I have both felt this pain at Stripe and Amazon, respectively, and that's why we’re building the validation layer for AI
